title: Sony forecasts lower gaming business sales amid memory price surge
content:
STORY: Sony forecast lower sales in its gaming business due to a memory chip price surge.

The tech giant said Friday annual sales in the segment would fall 6% to $28 billion.

It also blamed lower hardware sales as its PlayStation 5 ages.

Although the Japanese company said it expects gaming profit to rise 30%.

That's due to higher first-party software sales and no repeat of the impairment loss it recorded a year earlier.

Sony has been praised by analysts in recent years for its transformation into an entertainment powerhouse.

But markets have grown concerned about the impact of AI on its business, while Sony also faces a perceived lack of growth catalysts.

Both factors have weighed on shares in recent months.

Investors are also concerned about the impact of a memory-chip price surge.

As well as disruption to supply chains from the Iran war on margins at electronics manufacturers.

The firm said it sold 1.5 million PS5 consoles in the fourth quarter, which was a 46% drop on the same period a year earlier.

Looking ahead, the PS5 is expected to receive a major boost from the launch of the delayed "Grand Theft Auto VI."

The game is due to release in November.

Sony said in February it had secured the minimum quantity of memory needed to manage the year-end shopping season. 

Sony also said it sees higher profits at its pictures and chips units but a lower profit at its music business.

The group reported operating profit for the year ended March rose 13.4% to $9.2 billion - below analyst forecasts. 

Meanwhile, rival Nintendo said it expects to sell 16.5 million units of its Switch 2 console in the financial year ending March 2027.

It also projects profit to rise 2.7% to $2.36 billion.

But, like with Sony, investors are concerned about the possible impact of a memory price surge on margins.

title: Sony, Nintendo grapple with memory price surge as AI boom constrains supply
content: Nintendo and Sony both flagged the impact from surging memory prices on their games businesses on Friday, as the artificial intelligence boom constrains chip supply and deepens disruptions across the ‌tech sector.  "Super Mario" maker Nintendo said that higher component costs, particularly memory, and the impact of tariffs is expected to add roughly 100 billion yen ($638 million) to costs in the current financial year.  "The very fact that Nintendo felt compelled to act (price increase) suggests the rise in memory costs has become severe enough that it could no longer be absorbed internally - and, crucially, that there is little prospect of those cost pressures easing in the near ‌term," Morningstar analyst Kazunori Ito said.

title: Sony lifts profit targets even as PlayStation sales slide
content:
STORY: Sony on Thursday raised its full-year outlook after reporting a forecast-beating 22% jump in third quarter operating profit.

A robust performance from its image sensor and music divisions helped boost profit to $3.3 billion.

Shares in Sony climbed 1% on news of the results, but fell back to end the day broadly flat. 

The conglomerate has made a successful pivot from household electronics to entertainment.

But its share price has slid in recent months as investors question what its future growth drivers will be.

In one concern, sales of its PlayStation 5 console declined 16% from last year.

However, profit at the gaming unit grew by almost a fifth, helped by higher sales of software and a weaker yen.

The impact of AI is also a concern. 

A global tech investment boom sparked by the new tech has hardware makers grappling with surging memory chip prices.

It's also unclear what the adoption of artificial intelligence in video games will mean for the industry. 

Gaming stocks fell in recent days following the introduction of an AI-powered game-making tool by Alphabet's Google.
